title: Educational gender inequality in sub-Saharan Africa: a long-term perspective
markdown:
By **Joerg Baten, Michiel de Haas, Elisabeth Kempter, Felix Meier zu Selhausen**, 2023

**_Topic:**_ Education 

**_Keywords:**_ Education; Gender Inequality; Gender; Urbanisation; Africa

**_Africapolis data:**_ GIS, agglomeration data

**_Link to the original article:**_ [Educational gender inequality in sub-Saharan Africa: a long-term perspective] (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/padr.12430)
___

This study, by Baten, de Haas, Kempter and Meier zu Selhausen (2021) examines the **long-term patterns of educational gender disparities in sub-Saharan Africa**. We utilize a **birth-cohort approach** to cover most of the 20th century, connecting the colonial to the post-colonial era. Our analysis shows that as education expanded, gender gaps in sub-Saharan Africa first increased and then declined. 

In order to understand this phenomenon, we analyse gender gaps in education at three levels:  

1. comparing sub-Saharan Africa to other developing regions
2. comparing 21 African countries among themselves
3. investigating the correlates of educational gender inequality at the subnational level

###Sub-Saharan Africa compared to other developing regions 

At the start of the 20th century, gender disparities in terms of years of schooling were lower in sub-Saharan Africa compared to other developing regions including the Middle East and North Africa (MENA), South Asia (SA) and Southeast Asia (SEA). However, during the **colonial period**, **gender inequality increased in all regions**, peaking between 1.5 and 2.5 years of schooling. The tilting point for sub-Saharan Africa was later than for the other regions and **by the 1980s it had significant disparities** despite progress in closing the gap post-colonialism.

#####*Figure 1. Gender gaps in developing world regions over time*
![img](/images/tempimages/Baten_1_EN.jpeg)


###The Educational Gender Kuznets Curve 

To further analyse these trajectories, we express the **educational gender gap in relation to the number of years of education received by boys**. We discover an _inverted-U shaped pattern of gender gaps_ across different world regions, which we refer to as the _‘educational gender Kuznets curve.’_  The low gender gaps in schooling in the 1890s can be explained by the relatively low level of education. As **educational opportunities expanded**, families **prioritized investing in their sons’ education**, leading to an **increase in gender disparities**. However as male education reached 4 to 5 years, these disparities began to decrease. While sub-Saharan Africa performs comparatively well throughout its educational gender Kuznets curve, **it remains with a relatively high educational gender gap by the end of the 20th century due to Africa’s slow educational progress**. 

#####*Figure 2. Gender gaps in developing world regions in relation to male years of education* 
![img](/images/tempimages/Baten_2_EN.jpeg)


The **_‘educational gender Kuznets curve’_** can also be observed at the **country level**. In almost all African countries included in our study, educational gender gaps follow the pattern of an _inverted U-shaped trajectory_, with **disparities increasing until the mid-20th century and then levelling off or decreasing**. However, **southern African countries** including South Africa, Botswana, Lesotho, and to a lesser extent, Zimbabwe, had notably **low or even reversed gender gaps throughout the 20th century**. Reasons therefore may include **male absenteeism** due to cattle herding and **male labor migration for mining**. Women instead were left behind and may have benefited from their immobility in terms of attending school.

###Correlates of the Educational Gender Gap 

At the **subnational level, this study analyses the factors that may have shaped educational gender disparities**. We apply the _Least Squares Dummy Variable (LSDV)_ estimator and create *short birth-cohort panels* for the early _(1920-1939)_, late _(1940-1959)_ and postcolonial period _(1960-1979)_. Regression results show that **areas located on the coast**, with better **connectivity** and access to **railroads** as well as more **urbanised regions** enjoyed **higher gender equality in education**. This means that **African girls benefited from increased openness throughout the 20th century**. Moreover, we observe **lower educational gender gaps** in districts where **Christian missionary presence** was given, even after the late colonial period when mission schools lost their monopoly in British Africa ([Frankema 2012](https://academic.oup.com/ereh/article/16/4/335/468311) ; [Cogneau & Moradi 2014](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/journal-of-economic-history/article/borders-that-divide-education-and-religion-in-ghana-and-togo-since-colonial-times/4E82213AB62DF0B86451199AC279D13A); [Meier zu Selhausen 2019](https://www.econstor.eu/bitstream/10419/242677/1/aehn-wp-48.pdf)).

###Africapolis and the Role of Urbanisation 

We make use of the _Africapolis database_ to understand **how urban agglomeration and the presence of cities correlates with educational gender gaps in sub-Saharan Africa**. 

Existing literature shows that urbanisation and increased trade led to new job opportunities and resulted in a higher demand of labor in urban areas, making it more desirable to educate girls (Elkan 1957; Boserup 1970; De Haas and Frankema 2018).  

As a proxy for urbanisation, we consider the **share of urban population per birth region**. Therefore, we take into account all **cities per birth region** which have more than **10,000 inhabitants** and are provided by the _Africapolis database_ ([Africapolis, OECD/SWAC 2018](https://africapolis.org/en/data?country=Angola&keyfigure=totalPop&type=abs&year=2015)). Since we are interested in the **dynamic effect of urbanisation on educational gender inequality**, we construct our measure, _log city population (>10,000 inhabitants) per region for the 1920s, 1950s and 1970s_. Findings show that **urban agglomeration in sub-Saharan Africa was consistently associated with lower gender disparities in education**, although statistical significance is given only for the early colonial period.

#####*Map 1. Africapolis: Urban agglomeration in sub-Saharan Africa during the 1920s and the 1970s* 
![img](/images/tempimages/Baten_3_EN.jpeg)

*The views, conclusions and materials of external contributors do not represent the views of OECD/SWAC or its Members.*

_Boserup, E. (1970). [Woman's role in economic development.] (https://www.routledge.com/Womans-Role-in-Economic-Development/Boserup-Tan-Toulmin/p/book/9781844073924) London: George Allen & Unwin._ 

_Cogneau, D., & Moradi, A. (2014). [Borders that divide: Education and religion in Ghana and Togo since colonial times.] (https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/journal-of-economic-history/article/borders-that-divide-education-and-religion-in-ghana-and-togo-since-colonial-times/4E82213AB62DF0B86451199AC279D13A) The Journal of Economic History, 74(3), 694-729._

_Elkan, W. (1957). [The employment of women in Uganda.] (https://catalogue.leidenuniv.nl/primo-explore/search?vid=UBL_V1&search_scope=lib_asc&query=any,contains,9939073820002711) Bulletin-Inter-African Labour Institute, 4(4), 8-23._ 

_Frankema, E. H. (2012). [The origins of formal education in sub-Saharan Africa: was British rule more benign?.] (https://academic.oup.com/ereh/article/16/4/335/468311) European review of economic history, 16(4), 335-355._

###*Authors’ biographies* 

[**Joerg Baten**] (https://uni-tuebingen.de/de/142169) is professor of economic history at the University of Tuebingen. His research focuses on the long-run development of human capital, inequality, living standards and violence. His seven books (partly co-edited) include [_“A History of the Global Economy”_ (2016)](https://www.cambridge.org/highereducation/books/a-history-of-the-global-economy/99BA25553B6E1DBECAE815BA7FDBCD3A#overview) and [_“The Backbone of Europe: Health, Diet, Work and Violence over Two Millenia”_ (2019)](https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/backbone-of-europe/F4E2FD1F6175BBF92AE49E9480AEB701). Baten is editor-in-chief of the journal [_“Economics and Human Biology”_ ](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/economics-and-human-biology) (Impact Factor 2.8); 2015-17 he was president of the European Historical Economics Society.

[**Michiel de Haas**](https://www.wur.nl/nl/personen/michiel-dr.-ma-michiel-de-haas.htm)  is an Assistant Professor at the [_Rural and Environmental History Group_](https://www.wur.nl/en/research-results/chair-groups/social-sciences/economic-and-environmental-history-group.htm). His research focuses on the economic history of sub-Saharan Africa, with expertise in rural East and Central Africa, Uganda in particular. His thematic interests include the long-term development of living standards, economic migration, social and economic inequality, as well as the determinants and impact of agricultural commercialization and primary commodity specialization.

**Elisabeth Kempter** is a postdoctoral researcher in the [Department of Economics and Management at the University of Luxembourg](https://wwwen.uni.lu/fdef/department_of_economics_and_management). Her research covers both, economic development in the long run as well as current socio-economic issues. More precisely, her research aims to understand the underlying role of education, health, migration and women empowerment in economic development with a particular focus on sub-Saharan Africa. 

**Felix Meier zu Selhausen** is an Assistant Professor at [Utrecht University’s Department of Economic and Social History](https://www.uu.nl/en/research/economic-and-social-history). His research interests are at the intersection of economic history, development economics and demography. In particular, his research focuses on the impact of international trade on development in Africa, the relationship between religion and socio-economic development and gender relations in urban and rural contexts.
